svg.text: As-is: Chorus on the spine ↔ Pulse off the spine — two patterns for one job (serve live state)
Pulse reads pre-written snapshots off the loop (14ms). Chorus crawl synthesizes on demand ON the loop (8–45s). Same need, opposite shape — that asymmetry is #3055.
Colour = on chorus-api's single event loop (red) vs off it (green/blue). Connects chorus-search-hierarchy.svg to the pulse producer/consumer inventory (pulse-service-design.md). Wren 2026-05-24.
CHORUS — synthesize on demand, ON the loop
chorus-api · :3340 · ONE Node event loop = the spine
Every read endpoint runs here. Synchronous work freezes cards / nudge / context-inject / search for all three roles.
GET /api/chorus/search — façade over 3 systems (see chorus-search-hierarchy.svg)
① keyword SYNC · on the loop · ② semantic (Ollama+LanceDB) async · ③ graph (Fuseki) async
#3051 fixed the LIKE-scan freeze (2.08s → 0.027s). The sync keyword path is still on the loop.
modes: fts/semantic/hybrid/unified — merged with RRF
GET /api/chorus/crawl/:domain — 11-collector snapshot, synthesized ON THE LOOP
collectCards · Rdf · Mentions · Spine · Owl · Infra · Feedback · CodeScan · CodeFiles · Logs · Alerts
#3054 bounded mentions+spine, but the post-await chunk is still sync on the loop: SPARQL/timeline
processing (~79K triples for photos) + computeTrust + timeline.sort.
crawl/photos = 44,961ms · crawl/blog = 9,330ms — traced live 15:52–16:06 (via #3058 timestamps)
GET /api/chorus/freshness — now cached 30s (#3060, shipped today)
Was the same disease (read 170MB log + COUNT on the loop, ~1.4s). Now request path 2–8ms. Already pulse-shaped.
Stores (shared, read by the endpoints above)
SQLite · index.db
1.1 GB · 1.26M messages
FTS5 + crawler summaries
(source=crawler, channel=crawl:*)
LanceDB · vectors
21 GB · 768-dim
async (off-thread)
Fuseki · SPARQL
:3030/pods · urn:gathering:*
async HTTP
The pattern: a reader hits the endpoint → chorus-api computes the whole answer right then, on the loop.
No pre-computed snapshot to serve. Cost is paid per request, synchronously, in front of everyone else's work.
freshness (#3060) just escaped this by caching. crawl has not — it is the last big on-loop synthesizer.
PULSE — read pre-written snapshots, OFF the loop
① Producers — each writes a file on its OWN cadence (separate processes)
role-state CLI → {role}-declared.json (on transition)
observer hook → {role}-inferred.json (per tool-use)
chorus-log → chorus.log (every spine event)
alert scripts → /tmp/alert-* (on fire)
nudge CLI → voice-inbox/* (on nudge)
deep-health.sh LaunchAgent → deep-health-latest.json (5min)
cards CLI → board-wip-snapshot.json (on mutation +10s TTL)
pulse → freshness-snapshot.json (30s cache of /freshness)
② chorus-hooks pulse (Rust)
Runs in the daemon, NOT chorus-api.
Reads the files → annotates each
section with age → exposes.
"Event-bus cache, not aggregator —
read, annotate, expose. No synth."
Fires on UserPromptSubmit +
post-tool-use. Cold 754ms / warm 14ms.
/tmp/pulse-latest.json
the pre-written snapshot · ~14ms warm read
③ Consumers — read the snapshot, never compute
• context_inject.rs — shapes pulse into the per-prompt boot envelope (regen if >30s old)
• tiles.ts — Clearing UI role/board/health/alerts tiles
• GET /api/chorus/pulse/latest — chorus-api re-serves the file verbatim (read-through)
• agents (Claude sessions) — whole file into boot prompt
Why pulse is fast and never freezes the spine
The heavy work (assembling state) happens in a SEPARATE process on its own cadence and lands in a file.
The read path just opens that file. No producer can block a consumer; no consumer triggers a producer.
This is the producer → snapshot file → read-through pattern. It is the shape chorus crawl is missing.
Note: GET /api/chorus/pulse/latest already proves chorus-api CAN be a pure read-through —
it just re-serves the file pulse wrote. crawl should serve a file the same way.
context_inject reads BOTH per prompt:
pulse file (off-loop 14ms) + /search (on-loop). Two patterns.
pulse's freshness section = 30s cache of chorus /freshness →
THE INVERSION — the "background" crawl job is the biggest CAUSE of on-loop crawl work
com.chorus.crawler-index LaunchAgent (every 30min, #3054-throttled) → index-crawler-snapshots.sh → loops 27 domains → curl GET /api/chorus/crawl/:domain
So a process that LOOKS off-loop actually forces chorus-api to run the 8–45s synthesis 27 times every half hour, on the loop — then stores only a short text
summary into SQLite (source=crawler) for search to find. The full snapshot is computed, used once for a summary, and thrown away.
Compare pulse: its producers compute IN THEIR OWN PROCESS and write a reusable snapshot file. The crawl job calls the loop instead of being a producer.
Result: the crawl freezes traced today (photos 45s, blog 9s) line up with this cadence job + any live reader of /crawl — both pay the on-loop cost.
#3055 — make crawl a Pulse-style producer (don't invent infra; invert the existing one)
1. A standalone producer (run by the SAME existing launchd cadence job) computes each domain's snapshot in its own process — off chorus-api's loop —
importing the 11 collectors directly, and writes the full snapshot to ~/.chorus/crawl-snapshots/<domain>.json (the pulse /tmp/*.json file convention).
2. GET /api/chorus/crawl/:domain becomes a read-through — serve the snapshot file (~5ms, like /pulse/latest), annotate age. Cold/never-crawled → "warming", never a live 45s synth.
3. Reuses what already exists: the launchd cadence, the 27-domain loop, per-domain status tracking. No new SQLite table, no new launchd job. Touches Silas's lane (the job).
Net: crawl stops freezing the spine the way freshness (#3060) and pulse already don't. The diagram's two halves become one pattern.
